Title: Pictures at an Exhibition
Tags: nationalportraitgallery london art gallery exhibition painting npg
Description:
Visited the National Portrait Gallery in London with a friend. Been there many times, and it was really just a place to chill and chat. It has free entry, which I really value. It's good for the soul, and I'd be more than happy for a small city tax levied on hotel stays to help fund these galleries.

National Portrait Gallery, London WC2

Description: The Art of Banksy Exhibition in London. Banksy is a pseudonymous England-based street artist, political activist and film director whose real name and identity remain unconfirmed and the subject of speculation. Active since the 1990s, his satirical street art and subversive epigrams combine dark humour with graffiti executed in a distinctive stencilling technique. His works of political and social commentary have appeared on streets, walls and bridges throughout the world. His work grew out of the Bristol underground scene, which involved collaborations between artists and musicians. Banksy says that he was inspired by 3D, a graffiti artist and founding member of the musical group Massive Attack. Banksy displays his art on publicly visible surfaces such as walls and self-built physical prop pieces. He no longer sells photographs or reproductions of his street graffiti, but his public "installations" are regularly resold, often even by removing the wall they were painted on. A small number of Banksy's works are officially, non-publicly, sold through an agency named Pest Control. Information Source: en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banksy

Title: The John-Furnish family at home
Tags: portrait nationalportraitgallery npg london catherineopie eltonjohn davidfurnish lifeathome teenager teenageboys adolescent blacklabrador books bookshelves library shuggiebain ©catherineopie ©nationalportraitgallery samsunggalaxys24 boysinjeans
Description: Any trip to London is incomplete without my dropping into the National Portrait Gallery to see what’s new – and my visit this time was particularly well-rewarded: the new family portrait of Elton John and David Furnish by Catherine Opie had just been unveiled. Opie is an American fine art photographer and teacher who likes to ‘document the relationship between the individual and the space inhabited’ – in this case Sir Elton John and his husband David Furnish with their sons Elijah and Zachary. They were photographed (along with their labradors Joseph and Jacob) in the Library of their home in Old Windsor last year. They are surrounded by books ranging in subjects from the Krays to David Bailey, Tom Stoppard to Shuggie Bain (one of my own particular favourites) and everything else in between. For me, this family portrait is beautifully taken, and the warmth and trust between the photographer and her subjects helps to create a relaxed result, there for all to see. A close-up of Elton is here.

Title: Angus McEvoy
Tags: bronzebust npg nationalportraitgallery london england angusmcevoy artist painter portraitpainter portraitist sculpture francisderwentwood warartist ©peterdenton canoneos200d
Description:
This is Angus McEvoy (1877-1927) who, in his day, was a fashionable portrait painter. He trained at the Slade School of Art, then during the First World War he was an official war artist attached to the Royal Navy. 

McEvoy established his reputation with paintings of landscapes and interiors and later enjoyed success as a portrait painter, mainly of women. 

This rather beautiful bronze bust, at the National Portrait Gallery in London, was created in 1915 by Francis Derwent Wood.

Title: Owen Cooper at the National Portrait Gallery
Tags: owencooper actor adolescence netflix tv tvdrama television televisiondrama erindoherty dominicwhisson npg nationalportraitgallery london england taylorwessing ©peterdenton ©dominicwhisson emmy goldenglobe criticschoice photography reflective reflection reflet portrait samsunggalaxys24 pictureframe
Description: Britain’s undoubted ‘breakout star’ of 2025 was 15-year-old Owen Cooper, whose astonishing acting performance in Adolescence has received global plaudits and awards. Adolescence was a four-part Netflix drama about a schoolboy accused of murder. Each episode was shot in real time and in one take – a remarkable achievement by everyone concerned, not least the technical crew and the actors, none of whom could afford to make a single mistake. In particular, Owen’s two-hander with Erin Doherty in the third episode was gripping, flawless television. This portrait of Owen Cooper, by Dominic Whisson, was one of the highlights of the Taylor Wessing Portrait exhibition at the National Portrait Gallery in London. The annual prize showcases the work of young photographers, gifted amateurs and established professionals. This year's exhibition was one of the best I've seen (and I've seen them all!) Owen is captured here in reflective mood – which gave me the idea for my own theme.

Title: Piccadilly Circus Underground Station
Description:
Charles Holden station model in former ticket office window.

Charles Henry Holden (1875–1960) is widely regarded as the architect who defined the visual identity of modern London through his collaboration with Frank Pick, the General Manager of the Underground Electric Railways Company of London (UERL). His work on the Morden extension of the City and South London Railway (now the Northern Line) between 1924 and 1926 marked a pivotal shift in transport architecture, moving away from the decorative "ox-blood" terracotta tiles of Leslie Green toward a stripped-back, functionalist modernism.

The production of physical models for the Morden extension stations was not merely a stylistic choice but a functional necessity driven by the specific demands of Frank Pick and the constraints of the urban sites. Holden produced these models for several critical reasons:

1. Visualisation of the "Folding Screen" Concept

Holden’s primary innovation for the Morden extension was the "folding screen" façade. This design featured a double-height ticket hall clad in Portland stone, with large glazed screens divided by stone columns. Because these stations were often located on awkward, restricted corner sites, Holden used models to demonstrate how this standardised "unit" of design could be "folded" or adapted to fit different street angles without losing its brand identity. The models allowed Pick and the UERL board to see how the three-dimensional Underground roundel, which Holden had integrated into the stonework of the capitals, would appear from various street-level perspectives.

2. Frank Pick’s "Total Design" Philosophy

Frank Pick was a proponent of the Design and Industries Association (DIA) philosophy, which emphasized "fitness for purpose." Pick was notoriously meticulous and demanded to see how buildings would integrate with their surroundings and how natural light would penetrate the interior spaces. Holden’s models were essential tools for simulating the play of light through the massive glazed screens, ensuring the ticket halls would be "bright underground spaces" that felt welcoming to commuters.

3. Replacing the "Unsatisfactory" Designs of Stanley Heaps

Prior to Holden’s appointment, the UERL’s in-house architect, Stanley Heaps, had submitted designs for the Morden extension that Pick found "unsatisfactory" and lacking in modern character. Holden was brought in specifically to provide a more sophisticated, international style. The production of detailed models served as a "proof of concept" to reassure the railway directors that Holden’s radical departure from traditional station aesthetics—using plain Portland stone and geometric massing—would be both prestigious and durable.

The models also helped Holden refine the "kit of parts" that would eventually define his "Holdenesque" style. By working in three dimensions, Holden could coordinate the placement of internal fixtures—such as the "passimeter" (the central ticket booth), clocks, and signage—with the external architecture. This holistic approach ensured that the flow of passengers from the street to the platforms was intuitive, a hallmark of his later "brick box" designs like Sudbury Town.

The success of the Morden extension, validated through these early models and final constructions, established Holden as the "Wren of the 20th Century" for London. The seven stations—Clapham South, Balham, Tooting Bec, Tooting Broadway, Colliers Wood, South Wimbledon, and Morden—remain largely as Holden designed them, protected as listed buildings due to their pioneering role in the Modern Movement in Britain.

Title: Piccadilly Circus Underground Station
Description:
The roundel tower poster in former ticket office window.

Charles Holden’s architectural contributions to the London Underground during the 1920s and 1930s represent a pivotal shift from Edwardian decorative styles to a functionalist, modernist aesthetic. The incorporation of prominent towers—often referred to as "Roundel Towers" or "Beacon Towers"—at stations such as Boston Manor, Osterley, and Sudbury Town was a deliberate urban planning strategy designed to create a "landmark" presence in the rapidly expanding suburban "Metroland."

During the interwar period, the London Underground was extending into undeveloped or newly residential areas of West and North London. Holden, working under the patronage of Frank Pick, the Managing Director of the Underground Electric Railways Company of London (UERL), sought to create buildings that were not merely functional transit points but civic anchors.

The "Roundel Tower" served as a vertical marker in a horizontal suburban landscape. At Boston Manor, the tower is a tall, slender brick structure that rises significantly above the low-profile ticket hall. This verticality was intended to make the station visible from a distance, acting as a "beacon" for commuters navigating the suburban streets.The tower at Boston Manor is particularly noted for its "fin" of glass bricks—a vertical strip that was illuminated from within at night. This lighting feature transformed the station into a literal lighthouse for the community, reinforcing the Underground’s identity as a modern, reliable, and omnipresent service.

Holden’s design philosophy was deeply influenced by a 1930 tour of Continental Europe he took with Frank Pick. During this trip, they studied the works of Willem Marinus Dudok in the Netherlands and Gunnar Asplund in Sweden. Dudok’s Hilversum Town Hall, with its dramatic asymmetrical massing and prominent clock tower, provided a direct precedent for the use of vertical brick towers to balance horizontal volumes.

Holden adapted these European "International Style" elements into what became known as the "Holdenesque" style. This involved:

Fitness for Purpose: A belief that the building's form should be dictated by its function.

Geometric Purity: The use of simple shapes—cubes, cylinders, and rectangles.

Unadorned Surfaces: Moving away from the "ox-blood" terracotta tiles of the earlier Leslie Green era towards plain brick and Portland stone.

The towers were specifically designed to display the London Underground "roundel"—the red circle and blue bar logo. By placing the roundel high upon a tower, Holden and Pick integrated corporate branding with monumental architecture. At Boston Manor and Osterley, the towers are topped with these symbols, ensuring that the brand was the highest point in the local skyline.

While Holden was the primary consultant, some stations like Boston Manor and Osterley involved significant input from the UERL’s internal architect, Stanley Heaps. Heaps followed Holden’s "brick box with a concrete lid" template but often emphasized the tower element even more dramatically to compensate for the stations' locations near large depots or industrial sites.

The use of the tower also allowed for the integration of modern materials. The "Roundel Towers" were not just masonry; they utilised reinforced concrete frames clad in brick. The inclusion of vertical glazing or glass bricks allowed light to pour out at night, a technique Holden called "architecture of the night." This was a psychological tool to make the Underground feel safe, modern, and high-tech.

In summary, Holden incorporated these towers to serve as urban signposts, to project a sense of civic permanence in new suburbs, and to elevate the corporate identity of London Transport to the level of high art through the lens of European Modernism.

Title: Piccadilly Circus Underground Station
Description:
Frank Pick poster in former ticket office window.

Frank Pick (1878–1941) was a pioneering British transport administrator and design theorist who served as the Managing Director of the Underground Electric Railways Company of London (UERL) and later as the first Chief Executive Officer of the London Passenger Transport Board (LPTB). Often described as the "Maecenas of our time" and compared to urban visionaries like Baron Haussmann and Robert Moses, Pick is credited with transforming London’s disparate transport services into a world-class, integrated network with a cohesive corporate identity. His philosophy centered on the "fitness for use," arguing that utility and beauty were inseparable in public service.

Frank Pick was born on 23 November 1878 in Spalding, Lincolnshire, the eldest of five children to Francis Pick, a draper, and Fanny Clarke. A studious child, Pick preferred collecting moths and reading to athletics. He attended St Peter’s School in York on a scholarship and was later articled to a solicitor, George Crombie, in 1897. Although he qualified as a solicitor in 1902 and earned a law degree from the University of London, he never practiced law.

In 1902, Pick joined the North Eastern Railway (NER), working in the traffic statistics department. His analytical prowess caught the attention of the NER’s general manager, Sir George Gibb, who appointed Pick as his assistant in 1904. When Gibb moved to London in 1906 to manage the Underground Electric Railways Company of London (UERL), he invited Pick to accompany him.

By 1908, Pick had become the publicity officer for the UERL. Working alongside General Manager Albert Stanley (later Lord Ashfield), he began addressing the financial instability of the tube lines by focusing on marketing and passenger experience. Pick believed that a unified brand would reduce confusion and encourage off-peak travel.

Pick sought a visual language that was "unmistakably of the twentieth century." In 1913, he commissioned the calligrapher Edward Johnston to create a new sans-serif typeface. The resulting "Johnston" typeface, introduced in 1916, remains the standard for London’s transport today. Pick also refined the "bulls-eye" logo—a red disc with a blue horizontal bar—which Johnston redesigned into the modern "roundel" in 1919.

Pick transformed station environments by standardising poster sizes and locations to prevent a "crowded jumble" that obscured station names. He commissioned avant-garde artists such as Edward McKnight Kauffer, Paul Nash, and Graham Sutherland to create posters that promoted the Underground as a gateway to the countryside and cultural attractions. This initiative turned the Underground into a "poster gallery for the people."

In 1924, Pick commissioned architect Charles Holden to design new stations, seeking a "new architectural idiom" that rejected traditional ornamentation in favor of functionalism. Following a 1930 tour of modern architecture in Germany, the Netherlands, and Scandinavia, Pick and Holden developed the "Sudbury Town" prototype—a "brick box with a concrete lid" that maximised natural light and passenger flow.

Holden’s designs for the Piccadilly line extensions (such as Arnos Grove and Southgate) and the LPTB headquarters at 55 Broadway became landmarks of British Modernism. Pick’s attention to detail extended to every element of the station environment, including "passimeter" ticket booths, uplighters for escalators, and even the moquette fabrics used for bus and train upholstery.

The creation of the London Passenger Transport Board (LPTB) in 1933 marked the culmination of Pick and Ashfield’s efforts to unify London’s transport. As Chief Executive and Vice-Chairman, Pick managed the integration of five railway companies, fourteen municipal tramways, and dozens of bus operators. Under his leadership, the "New Works Programme" (1935–1940) was launched to modernise and extend the network, including the electrification of the Metropolitan line and the expansion of the Central and Northern lines.

Pick’s tenure was characterised by a rigorous, fortnightly management cycle and a prodigious workload. He was known for his "Nonconformist conscience" and a demanding nature that earned him the nickname "Jonah." Despite his success, he declined both a knighthood and a peerage, preferring to remain a "benevolent dictator" of design and administration.

As World War II approached, Pick prepared the transport plan for the mass evacuation of civilians from London in 1939. He retired from the LPTB in May 1940 following disagreements with the board regarding government-imposed dividend limits. He briefly served as the Director-General of the Ministry of Information but resigned after four months due to the political nature of the role.

Pick spent his final months studying the wartime use of Britain's canals and rivers. He died of a cerebral hemorrhage at his home in Golders Green on 7 November 1941.

Frank Pick is remembered as the "ideal patron" of the arts and a master of public administration. His influence is visible in the diagrammatic Tube map designed by Harry Beck (which Pick initially hesitated to adopt but eventually championed) and the enduring "Underground" brand. In 2016, on the 75th anniversary of his death, a permanent memorial titled Beauty < Immortality was unveiled at Piccadilly Circus station to honour his contribution to the city.

Title: Piccadilly Circus Underground Station
Description:
Frank Pick Memorial

The Frank Pick memorial, titled Beauty < Immortality, is a permanent site-specific installation located in the ticket hall of Piccadilly Circus Underground station. Unveiled on November 7, 2016, to mark the 75th anniversary of Pick’s death, the memorial serves to honour the man who transformed London’s transport network into a world-renowned paragon of integrated design and corporate identity.

Frank Pick was a solicitor by training who joined the Underground Electric Railways Company of London (UERL) in 1906. Despite having no formal background in art or architecture, he became the primary architect of the "Golden Age" of transport design.As the Managing Director of the London Underground and later the Vice-Chairman of the London Passenger Transport Board (LPTB), Pick commissioned the most influential designers of the 20th century to create a unified aesthetic for the city.

His legacy includes:

The Johnston Typeface: Commissioned from Edward Johnston in 1913 to ensure transport signage was distinct from commercial advertising.

The Roundel: The iconic "bulls-eye" logo, refined by Johnston under Pick’s direction in 1919.
Modernist Architecture: The collaboration with architect Charles Holden, resulting in landmark stations like 55 Broadway and the Piccadilly line extensions.
The Tube Map: The adoption of Harry Beck’s schematic diagram in 1933, which revolutionised urban navigation.

The memorial was commissioned by the London Transport Museum and Art on the Underground as the finale of the "Transported by Design" program. The project was awarded to the BAFTA-winning and Turner Prize-nominated artist duo Ben Langlands and Nikki Bell.

The artists spent a year researching the London Transport Museum archives in Acton. During their research, they discovered a "doodle" in the margins of Pick’s personal papers—specifically, notes he had scrawled while waiting to deliver a speech. This note contained a series of eight words arranged in two columns, connected by arrows, which the artists interpreted as Pick’s personal philosophy on the transformative power of design.

The "equation" found in his notes was:
Beauty←Immortality
Utility←Perfection
Goodness←Righteousness
Truth←Wisdom

Langlands and Bell viewed these words as a "pre-speech pep talk" or a mantra that suggested Pick believed changing one's surroundings could fundamentally change one's life.

The memorial is situated on the arching outer wall of the Piccadilly Circus ticket hall, occupying a space where a bank of obsolete public telephone boxes once stood. The location is highly symbolic, as Piccadilly Circus was considered one of Charles Holden’s greatest triumphs and a "pièce de résistance" of Pick’s tenure.

The installation features:

Bronze Lettering: The eight words from Pick’s notes are cast in solid bronze letters, each 2 meters high, using the Johnston Typeface.

The Roundel: A large, backlit version of the London Underground roundel is included, with "Frank Pick" inscribed in the central blue bar instead of a station name.

Materials: To ensure the work harmonised with the Grade II listed station, the artists used travertine stone tiles (installed by the specialist firm Szerelmey), bronze, and enamel—materials consistent with the station’s original 1920s Art Deco decorative scheme.

Significance and Legacy

The memorial was funded in part by a public crowdfunding campaign, reflecting Pick’s status as a "forgotten hero" whose work is seen by millions daily but whose name remained largely unknown to the general public. At the unveiling, Sam Mullins, Director of the London Transport Museum, noted that the memorial returns Pick to a central position in the identity of London.

By utilising the very tools Pick championed—the Johnston font and the roundel—Langlands and Bell "gave Pick his words back," creating a tribute that functions both as a piece of contemporary art and a historical marker of the man who believed that "London Transport is or will be a work of art."

Title: St Martin in the Fields
Tags: london stmartininthefields church
Description:
St Martin-in-the-Fields is a Church of England parish church at the north-east corner of Trafalgar Square in the City of Westminster, London. It is dedicated to Saint Martin of Tours. There has been a church on the site since at least the medieval period. It was at that time located in the farmlands and fields beyond the London wall, when it was awarded to Westminster Abbey for oversight.

It became a principal parish church west of the old City in the early modern period as Westminster's population grew. When its medieval and Jacobean structure was found to be near failure, the present building was constructed in an influential neoclassical design by James Gibbs in 1722–1726. The church is one of the visual anchors adding to the open-urban space around Trafalgar Square.

Title: London Coliseum
Tags: london theatre coliseum
Description:
The London Coliseum (also known as the Coliseum Theatre) is a theatre in St Martin's Lane, Westminster, built as one of London's largest and most luxurious family variety theatres. Opened on 24 December 1904 as the London Coliseum Theatre of Varieties, it was designed by the architect Frank Matcham for the impresario Oswald Stoll. Their ambition was to build the largest and finest music hall, described as the "people's palace of entertainment" of its age.

At the time of construction, the Coliseum was one of the few theatres in Europe to provide lifts for taking patrons to the upper levels of the house, and was the first theatre in England to have a triple revolve installed on its stage. The theatre has 2,359 seats making it the largest theatre in London.

Title: Night of 13/14 April 1984
Tags: londonbuses thx564s daimlerfleetline dms
Description:
This was the night that London Buses were dragged into late 20th century reality. A reasonably comprehensive night bus network was introduced across London - instead of a random collection of heritage routes that mostly didn't run on Saturday night. Obviously you don't run buses on the busiest night!   
This was Trafalgar Square. The new centre of the network (rather than Fleet Street)!

Title: London 08 August 2025
Tags: england gb greatbritain unitedkingdom photograph gaslamp london vanishingpoint streets europe uk goodwinscourt lane street windows shops quaint
Description:
Goodwins Court. London: 
This is a great little street. Rumoured (only rumoured I think) to be the inspiration behind the Diagon Alley in the Harry Potter series by J.K. Rowling. 
More certain is that the BBC used it as a filming location for their version of “Bleak House” by Charles Dickens. 
And it has certainly had some famous residents: Thomas Holcroft, Thomas De Quincey and William Hazlitt.

Title: Lullaby: Madame Augustine Roulin Rocking a Cradle (La Berceuse)1889
Tags: arles london nationalgallery vincentvangogh art painting poetsandlovers vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description: While in Arles, Van Gogh painted Augustine Roulin, the wife of postal worker Joseph Roulin (35.1982), in bold, exaggerated colors against a vividly patterned floral background. The rope in her hands leads to a cradle beyond the confines of the frame. At right, the painter inscribed the title “La Berceuse,” which means both “lullaby” and “she who rocks the cradle.” The Roulins had a baby at home, but Van Gogh conceived of the action in a broader sense as well, writing to his brother that he would like to see this painting “in the cabin of a boat” where fishermen in “their melancholy isolation, exposed to all the dangers, alone on the sad sea…would experience a feeling of being rocked, reminding them of their own lullabies.” This painting is one of five variations Van Gogh painted on the same subject.

Title: The Large Plane Trees (Road Menders at Saint-Rémy), 1889
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting theclevelandmuseumofart vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description: The Large Plane Trees (Road Menders at Saint-Rémy), 1889 The Cleveland Museum of Art, Gift of the Hanna Fund, 1947.209.  Like Two Poplars in the Alpilles near Saint-Rémy (on view nearby), Vincent van Gogh painted this autumnal landscape while living at an asylum near Saint-Rémy in southern France where he was treated for severe depression. Understanding that painting from nature eased his symptoms, Van Gogh’s physician permitted the artist to paint landscapes outside of the asylum. Van Gogh described this painting in a letter to his brother Theo: “The last study I did is a view of the village, where they were at work under some enormous plane trees—repairing the pavements. . . . There are heaps of sand, stones, and the gigantic trunks—the leaves yellowing and here and there you can get a glimpse of a house front and small figures.”

Title: Field with Poppies, 1889
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting kunsthallebremen vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description: Field with Poppies, 1889 Kunsthalle Bremen - Der Kunstverein in Bremen.  At the beginning of May 1889, four and a half months after his serious breakdown, Vincent van Gogh entered an asylum in Saint- Rémy in the Provence. When he was allowed to leave the grounds of the institution at the beginning of June, Field with Poppies was one of the first motifs he decided to paint. From a slightly elevated vantage point, he depicts the view over the valley with grain fields: It is a tapestry of impastoed colors dominated by the contrasting effect of the complementary colors of red and green. The swirls of color lend the canvas dynamism, whereas the lines of perspective draw the viewer’s gaze into the distance. When Gustav Pauli acquired Field with Poppies for 30,000 marks, thus continuing with his progressive purchasing policy, he sparked an art scandal that has since become famous. The Worpswede painter Carl Vinnen initiated the “Protest of German Artists,” a pamphlet in which numerous artists and art critics came out against the purported “foreign infiltration of German art” and what in their opinion was an increased acquisition of French art by German museums—an accusation that was not borne out by the facts. Several months later, the “Answer to the Protest of German Artists” was published, to which prominent artists of the avant-garde contributed, such as August Macke, Franz Marc, Max Pechstein, and Vassily Kandinsky.

Title: The Yellow House (The Street) 1888
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting vangoghmuseum vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description:
The Yellow House (The Street) 1888 Van Gogh Museum, Amsterdam (Vincent van Gogh Foundation).  In May 1888, Van Gogh rented four rooms in a house on Place Lamartine in Arles (southern France). The green shutters in this painting of the square show where he lived. Shortly after moving into the ‘Yellow House’, he sent Theo a description and sketch of his painting of it: ‘it’s tremendous, these yellow houses in the sunlight and then the incomparable freshness of the blue.’

The work, which Van Gogh himself called ‘The Street’, records the artist’s immediate surroundings: he often ate at the restaurant on the left, and the home of his friend, the postman Joseph Roulin, lay just beyond the second railway bridge.

Vincent had finally found a place at the Yellow House where he could not only paint but also have his friends come to stay. His plan was to turn the yellow corner-building into an artists’ house, where like-minded painters could live and work together.

Shortly after moving into the ‘Yellow House’, he sent Theo a description and sketch of his painting of it: ‘it’s tremendous, these yellow houses in the sunlight and then the incomparable freshness of the blue.’

The work, which Van Gogh himself called ‘The Street’, records the artist’s immediate surroundings: he often ate at the restaurant on the left, and the home of his friend, the postman Joseph Roulin, lay just beyond the second railway bridge.

Vincent had finally found a place at the Yellow House where he could not only paint but also have his friends come to stay. His plan was to turn the yellow corner-building into an artists’ house, where like-minded painters could live and work together.

Title: Van Gogh's Chair, 1888
Tags: arles london nationalgallery vincentvangogh art painting poetsandlovers vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description:
Van Gogh's Chair, 1888 The National Gallery, London,  This painting of a simple chair set on a bare floor of terracotta tiles is one of Van Gogh’s most iconic images. It was painted in late 1888, soon after fellow artist Paul Gauguin had joined him in Arles in the south of France. The picture was a pair to another painting, Gauguin’s Chair (Van Gogh Museum, Amsterdam). They were to be hung together, with one chair turned to the right, the other to the left.

Both chairs function as surrogate portraits, representing the personalities and distinct artistic outlooks of the two artists. While Van Gogh’s chair is simple and functional, Gauguin’s is an elegant and finely carved armchair. Van Gogh’s chair, on which he placed his pipe and tobacco, is shown in bright daylight. Gauguin’s, with two novels on its seat, was painted at night and is illuminated by a candle and gas light.

Title: The Green Vineyard, 1888
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting kröllermüllermuseum otterlo vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description:
The Green Vineyard, 1888 Kröller-Müller Museum, Otterlo, The Netherlands. Fever for work
On 3 October 1888, Van Gogh writes to Paul Gauguin: ‘I have an extraordinary fever for work these days, at present I’m grappling with a landscape with blue sky above an immense green, purple, yellow vine with black and orange shoots. Little figures of ladies with red sunshades, little figures of grape-pickers with their cart further liven it up’.

Blood and tears
He paints The green vineyard in a single day in a vineyard near Montmajour, not far from Arles. Nature has been ‘exceptionally beautiful’ for a while and he wants to waste no time capturing it. His ‘study of the vineyards’ costs him a great deal of effort, as he later tells Theo: ‘I sweated blood and tears over it – but I have it’.

Unprecedented vitality
Van Gogh depicts the female figures and grape pickers in quick, varied brushstrokes under a bright blue sky with the lilac silhouette of Arles and orange roofs of the farms on the horizon. The paint is applied in a thick impasto, particularly in the foreground. The purple, red, yellow, light blue and orange touches of paint dance among the greenery and lend the work an unprecedented vitality.

Title: Self Portralt, 1889
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting nationalgalleryofart washington vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description: Self Portralt, 1889 National Gallery of Art, Washington, DC. Collection of Mr. and Mrs. John Hay Whitney, 1998.74.5 x11405.  This self–portrait is a particularly bold painting, apparently executed in a single sitting without later retouching. Here Van Gogh portrayed himself at work, dressed in his artist's smock with his palette and brushes in hand, a guise he had already adopted in two earlier self–portraits. While the pose itself and the intense scrutiny of the artist's gaze are hardly unique—one need but think of the occasionally uncompromising self–portraits of Rembrandt—the haunting and haunted quality of the image is distinct. The dark blue–violet of the smock and ground, the vivid orange of his hair and beard, create a startling contrast to the yellow and green of his face and heighten the gauntness of his features in a sallow complexion. The dynamic, even frenzied brushwork lends an uncommon immediacy and expressiveness to his portrayal. In its sheer intensity, it stands in sharp contrast to the other self–portrait he painted at the same time (Musée d'Orsay, Paris) in which the artist appears calmer and more self–possessed. Nevertheless, Van Gogh preferred the Washington painting as the one that captured his 'true character."

Title: The Bedroom 1889
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting artinstituteofchicago vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description: Art Institute of Chicago.  Vincent van Gogh so highly esteemed his bedroom painting that he made three distinct versions: the first, now in the collection of the Van Gogh Museum, Amsterdam; the second, belonging to the Art Institute of Chicago, painted a year later on the same scale and almost identical; and a third, smaller canvas in the collection of the Musée d’Orsay, Paris, which he made as a gift for his mother and sister. Van Gogh conceived the first Bedroom in October 1888, a month after he moved into his “Yellow House” in Arles, France. This moment marked the first time the artist had a home of his own, and he had immediately and enthusiastically set about decorating, painting a suite of canvases to fill the walls. Completely exhausted from the effort, he spent two-and-a-half days in bed and was then inspired to create a painting of his bedroom. As he wrote to his brother Theo, “It amused me enormously doing this bare interior. With a simplicity à la Seurat. In ﬂat tints, but coarsely brushed in full impasto, the walls pale lilac, the ﬂoor in a broken and faded red, the chairs and the bed chrome yellow, the pillows and the sheet very pale lemon green, the bedspread blood-red, the dressing-table orange, the washbasin blue, the window green. I had wished to express utter repose with all these very different tones.” Although the picture symbolized relaxation and peace to the artist, to our eyes the canvas seems to teem with nervous energy, instability, and turmoil, an effect heightened by the sharply receding perspective.

Title: Mountains at Saint-Rémy, 1889
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting guggenheimmuseum vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description: Mountains at Saint-Rémy, 1889 Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum, New York. Thannhauser Collection, Gift, Justin K. Thannhauser, 1978.  During the years preceding his suicide in 1890, Vincent van Gogh suffered increasingly frequent attacks of mental distress, the cause of which remains unclear. Mountains at Saint-Rémy was painted in July 1889, when Van Gogh was recovering from just such an episode at the hospital of Saint-Paul-de-Mausole in the southern French town of Saint-Rémy. The painting represents the Alpilles, a low range of mountains visible from the hospital grounds. In it, Van Gogh activated the terrain and sky with the heavy impasto and bold, broad brushstrokes characteristic of his late work.

Description: Olive Trees, 1889 Lent by the Minneapolis Institute of Art, Minneapolis. The Williarn Hood Dunwoody Fund.  This is one of fifteen canvases of olive trees that van Gogh executed between June and December of 1889. Earlier that year he had interned himself in the asylum of St-Paul, in the town of St-Rémy in southern France, where he would create his most profound works. The vibrant oranges and yellows suggest that the picture dates to the autumn months. Van Gogh left St-Rémy in May 1890, moving to Auvers, near Paris, where he continued to paint until his death by suicide in July.

Title: Tree Trunks in the Grass, 1890
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting kröllermüllermuseum vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description:
Kröller-Müller Museum
Close-up
During his Paris period, Van Gogh already painted a forest in an unusual and original way: not the tall trees and the foliage, but the ground, the base of the trunks and the low vegetation, such as ivy, grass and small plants. During his stay at the asylum in Saint-Rémy he again makes this type of ‘close-up’ of a piece of grassland with flowers and the trunks of two pine trees.

Powerful spatial effect
He paints with thick and thin lines, solid contours and small circles for the many flowers. The rough bark of the pine trees is rendered almost abstractly, while the grass field with white flowers and the yellow dandelions displays a certain sophistication. By emphasizing the foreground – the trees and the grass – and using a strong diagonal, a powerful spatial effect is created.

Untangled
He writes to his friend Emile Bernard: ‘I exaggerate, I sometimes make changes to the subject, but still I don’t invent the whole of the painting; on the contrary, I find it ready-made – but to be untangled – in the real world’.

Description: The Large Plane Trees (Road Menders at Saint-Rémy), 1889 The Cleveland Museum of Art, Gift of the Hanna Fund, 1947.209.  Like Two Poplars in the Alpilles near Saint-Rémy (on view nearby), Vincent van Gogh painted this autumnal landscape while living at an asylum near Saint-Rémy in southern France where he was treated for severe depression. Understanding that painting from nature eased his symptoms, Van Gogh’s physician permitted the artist to paint landscapes outside of the asylum. Van Gogh described this painting in a letter to his brother Theo: “The last study I did is a view of the village, where they were at work under some enormous plane trees—repairing the pavements. . . . There are heaps of sand, stones, and the gigantic trunks—the leaves yellowing and here and there you can get a glimpse of a house front and small figures.”

Title: Field with Poppies, 1889
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting kunsthallebremen vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description: Field with Poppies, 1889 Kunsthalle Bremen-Der Kunstverein in Bremen.  At the beginning of May 1889, four and a half months after his serious breakdown, Vincent van Gogh entered an asylum in Saint- Rémy in the Provence. When he was allowed to leave the grounds of the institution at the beginning of June, Field with Poppies was one of the first motifs he decided to paint. From a slightly elevated vantage point, he depicts the view over the valley with grain fields: It is a tapestry of impastoed colors dominated by the contrasting effect of the complementary colors of red and green. The swirls of color lend the canvas dynamism, whereas the lines of perspective draw the viewer’s gaze into the distance. When Gustav Pauli acquired Field with Poppies for 30,000 marks, thus continuing with his progressive purchasing policy, he sparked an art scandal that has since become famous. The Worpswede painter Carl Vinnen initiated the “Protest of German Artists,” a pamphlet in which numerous artists and art critics came out against the purported “foreign infiltration of German art” and what in their opinion was an increased acquisition of French art by German museums—an accusation that was not borne out by the facts. Several months later, the “Answer to the Protest of German Artists” was published, to which prominent artists of the avant-garde contributed, such as August Macke, Franz Marc, Max Pechstein, and Vassily Kandinsky.

Description: For Van Gogh, oleanders were joyous, life-affirming flowers that bloomed "inexhaustibly" and were always "putting out strong new shoots." In this painting of August 1888 the flowers fill a majolica jug that the artist used for other still lifes made in Arles. They are symbolically juxtaposed with Émile Zola's La joie de vivre, a novel that Van Gogh had placed in contrast to an open Bible in a Nuenen still life of 1885. Metropolitan Museum of Art

Title: Sunflowers1888 National Gallery, London, England
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting sunflowers england unitedkingdom vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description:
This is one of five versions of Sunflowers on display in museums and galleries across the world. Van Gogh made the paintings to decorate his house in Arles in readiness for a visit from his friend and fellow artist, Paul Gauguin.

‘The sunflower is mine’, Van Gogh once declared, and it is clear that the flower had various meanings for him. The different stages in the sunflower’s life cycle shown here, from young bud through to maturity and eventual decay, follow in the vanitas tradition of Dutch seventeenth-century flower paintings, which emphasise the transient nature of human actions. The sunflowers were perhaps also intended to be a symbol of friendship and a celebration of the beauty and vitality of nature.

The sunflower pictures were among the first paintings Van Gogh produced in Arles that show his signature expressive style. No other artist has been so closely associated with a specific flower, and these pictures are among Van Gogh’s most iconic and best-loved works.

Description: Vincent van Gogh painted this luminous image of sunflowers from memory, in the depths of winter in 1889. Throughout his ten-year career, Van Gogh painted sunflowers repeatedly in different arrangements and settings. The shapes, colors, and cheerfulness of the modest flower appealed to him. He associated its yellow color with sunshine, the south, and Christ, the light of the world. Over a single week in August 1888, Van Gogh painted four pictures of sunflowers, including a bold canvas of twelve sunflowers against a turquoise ground, now in the Neue Pinakothek, Munich. The speed of this work was driven by enthusiasm and necessity since the flowers were destined to wilt and fade. This painting is a variation on the work now in Munich. Far from being a simple copy, it is a new interpretation that gives each flower a pronounced personality.

Title: The Poet (Portrait of Eugène Boch), 1888
Tags: arles london nationalgallery poetsandlovers vincentvangogh art painting muséed’orsay eugèneboch vangogh βανγκογκ ποιητέσκαιεραστέσ εθνικήπινακοθήκη λονδίνο artexhibition έκθεσητέχνησ αρλ saintrémy σενρεμί southoffrance νότιαγαλλία postimpressionism μεταιμπρεσιονισμόσ ζωγραφική museum μουσείο thepoet οποιητήσ thelover οεραστήσ arthistory ιστορίατηστέχνησ culturalevent πολιτιστικήεκδήλωση
Description:
Van Gogh met the Belgian painter Eugène Boch (1855-1941) in mid-June 1888, while Boch was spending a few weeks near Arles. Around 8 July, Vincent mentioned Boch in a letter to his brother Théo: "I very much like the looks of this young man with his distinctive face, like a razor blade, and his green eyes".
On 11 August, his idea began to take shape: "I should like to paint the portrait of an artist friend, a man who dreams great dreams, who works like the nightingale sings, because it is his nature to do so. This man will be blond. I would like to convey in the picture my appreciation, the love that I have for him. So I will paint him just as he is, as faithfully as I can [...].
Behind his head, instead of painting the ordinary wall of this shabby appartment, I will paint infinity, I will do a simple background of the richest blue, the most intense blue that I can create, and through this simple combination of the bright head against this rich, blue background, I will obtain a mysterious effect, like a star in the depths of an azure sky".
Two weeks later, Boch sat for Van Gogh. "Well, thanks to him I have at last the first sketch of this painting that I have dreamt of for so long – The Poet. He sat for me. His fine head with his green eyes stands out in my portrait against a starry sky of deep ultramarine; he wears a short, yellow jacket, a collar of unbleached linen and a colourful tie".
Although he only considered it to be a "sketch", Van Gogh framed this work, which he called The Poet. We know that it hung for some time on the wall of his bedroom in the Yellow House, because it appears in the first version of The Bedroom (Amsterdam, Van Gogh Museum).

Title: A sideways take on Princess Margaret
Tags: london nationalportraitgallery thearts cecilbeaton princessmargaret royalty ballroomgown christiandior 1951 england europe europa fashionphotography vogue ©peterdenton samsunggalaxys24
Description: Cecil Beaton photographed Princess Margaret (younger sister of Queen Elizabeth II) for her 21st birthday in 1951, but not without controversy. According to Cecil Beaton’s Fashionable World – the first exhibition dedicated solely to the photographer’s ground-breaking fashion and portrait work, recently on display at the National Portrait Gallery – the ostentation of a Christian Dior-designed ballroom gown was not much liked in a post-war Britain where clothes rationing had not long ended. Nor was the fact that the princess chose a French couturier over a British designer. Such were the times. Beaton himself noted that Margaret ‘likes dressing up, flirtations, [and] going to nightclubs until all hours’. A hard-working royal, then.

Description: The National Gallery in London is staging an exhibition of the work of noted 18th century artist Joseph Wright of Derby. With just 9 paintings and a selection of mezzotints, it is rather small and hardly justifies the £12 entrance fee. However Wright's huge detailed and spectacularly lit canvasses deserve close inspection and reveal so much background detail as to make this show almost worth the fee.

Title: St Martin-in-the-Fields by Night
Tags: outdoor anglican architecture christian christianity church churchofengland england europe lateevening lighttrails london night winter англия архитектура зима лондон ночь церковь церковьанглии 伦敦 冬天 圣公宗 基督教 建筑 教堂 晚上 欧洲 英国 英国教会 英格兰
Description:
I was soooooo unbelievably proud of this photo when I took it about five weeks after buying my first DSLR!

St Martin-in-the-Fields is a Church of England parish church at the north-east corner of Trafalgar Square in the City of Westminster, London. Dedicated in honour of Saint Martin of Tours, there has been a church on the site since at least the medieval period. This location, at that time, was farmlands and fields beyond the London wall.

St Martin's became a principal parish church west of the old City in the early modern period as Westminster's population grew. When its medieval and Jacobean structure was found to be near failure, the present building was constructed in an influential neoclassical design by James Gibbs in 1722–1726. The church is one of the visual anchors adding to the open-urban space around Trafalgar Square.

This description incorporates text from the English Wikipedia.

Title: National Gallery, London
Tags: fairphone6 london nationalgallery borisanrep mosaic
Description: Mosaic Terrace Set into the floor of the first landing in the Gallery's Portico entrance is 'The Awakening of the Muses', a marble mosaic laid in 1933 by the Russian-born artist Boris Anrep (1885-1969). Between 1928 and 1933, the National Gallery commissioned Anrep to lay two mosaic pavements in the vestibule of the Main Hall to illustrate 'The Labours of Life' and 'The Pleasures of Life'. In 1952, Anrep laid a third pavement, 'The Modern Virtues'. The resulting mosaics are a celebration of everyday life, which lies underfoot in a busy public place. Anrep was an associate of the Bloomsbury Group of artists and writers, who notoriously championed modern art and modern attitudes. His Muses are not heavenly immortals, but portraits of people from his own world. Many of the characters are played by Anrep's Bloomsbury friends www.nationalgallery.org.uk/about-us/history/sculptures-an...

Title: Queen Elizabeth II - 16 November 2025
Tags: tz95p1004026sce queenelizabethii pietroannigoni nationalportraitgallery
Description: Queen Elizabeth II (1969) by Pietro Annigoni, tempera grassa on paper panel. Annigoni painted several portraits of Her Late Majesty, The Queen. This 1969 work 'Her Majesty in Robes of the British Empire' is dreadful and, quite properly, received criticism at the time. The use of space on the panel is poor and the head of Her Majesty appears to be added on to a mannequin. The proportions are all wrong; I don't know what the artist was thinking or seeing. Description: Piccadilly Circus London. Can get very very busy during the spring and summer months. There is or at least was an urban legend that said if you sit in Piccadilly Circus all day you will see someone you know! I remember sitting there in 1979 with a girlfriend and we bumped into a friend called Graham who worked in the same hospital as us 75 miles away on the South Coast of England! The statue is of the Greek God Anteros who over the years has been mistakenly known as the Greek God Eros. I really do love London. I took this photo with my Canon DSLR camera. I have added the link to Wiki below for anyone who would like to know more. en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Piccadilly_Circus#In_popular_culture

Title: Painting on exhibition inside The National Gallery, London
Tags: art nationalgallery trafalgarsquare london painting artmuseum sonya6700 viltrox35mmf17 dxophotolab dxo cs5
Description:
The National Gallery is an art museum in Trafalgar Square, London. Founded in 1824, it houses a collection of more than 2,300 paintings dating from the mid-13th century to 1900. The current director of the National Gallery is Gabriele Finaldi.
The National Gallery is an exempt charity, and a non-departmental public body of the Department for Culture, Media and Sport. Its collection belongs to the government on behalf of the British public, and entry to the main collection is free of charge.
Paintings exhibited include van Eyck’s “The Arnolfini Portrait”, Holbein’s “Ambassadors”, Monet’s “The Water-Lily Pond”, Constable’s “The Hay Wain” and Van Gogh’s “Sunflowers”. Other visitor favourites include works by Turner, Leonardo, Velázquez, Titian, Botticelli, Caravaggio and Vermeer.

Description: Piccadilly Circus London. Very empty for an August afternoon! The statue is of the Greek God Anteros who over the years has been mistakenly known as the Greek God Eros. London like many other cities had been badly effected by Covid-19. I took this photo with my Canon DSLR camera during the break from lockdown in August 2020. Description: We were waiting under a canopy, to meet our guide, for a tour of music venues and pubs in Soho. We have been told he will be carrying an open umbrella. Anyways...the group all met up and 9 of us had a slightly damp but thoroughly enjoyable walk through Soho, with a chance to dry out in the four interesting pubs we stopped in. Taken with my Fujifilm X-T30 II camera and a XF18-55mm lens. London.

Title: Fancy a Poke?
Tags: fishwithattitude pokehouse coventgarden restaurantfront winkwink foodielondon bilingualcaption londoneats streetphotography londonspots playfulplaces fancyapoke pinkfacade london restaurant urbandetail innuendoincluded
Description:
Bright coral walls, cheeky signage, and a come-hither glow from Covent Garden’s Poke House. No need to book—just walk in and let them poke you gently with sesame, soy and spicy mayo. A fishy little spot with delicious intentions and a name that invites endless innuendo.

Des murs corail, une enseigne espiègle, et un halo tentateur venu de Covent Garden. Voici le Poke House : pas besoin de réserver, entrez et laissez-vous piquer… doucement, avec du sésame, du soja et une touche de mayo épicée. Un coin savoureux au nom délicieusement suggestif.

Title: The Odd Couple – Felix and Oscar’s West End Rumble
Tags: drôlededuo classicplay classiccomedy theatre haymarketlegends clashofpersonalities theatreroyalhaymarket billkenwright neilsimon theatreposter theoddcouple westendtheatre play westendhistory théâtrelondres westendcomedy haymarkethistory posterarchives jackklugman broadwaytowestend comédieusàlondres tonyrandall
Description: This original poster from the Theatre Royal Haymarket marks the reunion of television’s iconic duo Tony Randall and Jack Klugman in Neil Simon’s classic comedy The Odd Couple—a strictly limited London season that thrilled fans on both sides of the Atlantic. Randall and Klugman famously originated their roles as neat-freak Felix Ungar and the gloriously slobbish Oscar Madison in the long-running US sitcom based on Simon’s 1965 Broadway hit. Their real-life rapport translated effortlessly to the Haymarket stage, where their finely tuned timing and mismatched chemistry brought new life to this tale of unlikely domesticity and friendly dysfunction. Supported by a strong cast including Trevor Bannister, Rodney Bewes, and Henry McGee, this West End run (presented by Bill Kenwright) proved that comedy gold doesn’t tarnish—it just relocates. ________________________________________________ Production Details of The Odd Couple at the Theatre Royal Haymarket Performance Dates: The Odd Couple was performed at the Theatre Royal Haymarket from June 26 to October 5, 1996. Starring: Tony Randall as Felix Ungar Jack Klugman as Oscar Madison Overview This production marked the reunion of Randall and Klugman, who had previously portrayed their characters in the iconic TV adaptation of Neil Simon's popular play. This version was directed by Harvey Medlinsky and showcased the duo's remarkable chemistry in a classic story about mismatched roommates—Felix, the neatnik, and Oscar, the slovenly sportswriter. The Play's Background The Odd Couple, originally written by Neil Simon, focuses on the comedic friction between two divorced men who decide to live together. The play premiered on Broadway in 1965, featuring Walter Matthau and Art Carney. The dynamic between Felix and Oscar has been adapted into films and other formats since its inception. Theatrical Context The Theatre Royal Haymarket, one of London's oldest venues, has a rich history and was once the site of innovative performances, including the first scheduled matinée. The production in 1996 maintained the show's traditional comedic style, without major alterations, allowing Randall and Klugman's interpretations to shine.

Title: Lady Windermere’s Fan – Wilde Whispers and Drawing Room Daggerwork
Tags: westendhistory théâtrelondres theatreroyalhaymarket comédievictorienne westendtheatre fanandfury vanessaredgrave ladywindermeresfan wilderevival londontheatre play theatreposter peterhallcompany theatre oscarwilde rumeursetrévélations haymarkethistory classicplay wildewomen joelyrichardson
Description:
This beautifully preserved poster from the Theatre Royal Haymarket announces the Peter Hall Company’s revival of Lady Windermere’s Fan by Oscar Wilde, starring a powerhouse cast: Vanessa Redgrave, Joely Richardson, Jack Davenport, John McCallum, Googie Withers, and David Yelland.

Wilde’s deliciously sharp 1892 comedy, which skewers upper-class hypocrisy and double standards, was given a luxurious and emotionally intelligent staging by Sir Peter Hall, with sets by John Gunter and lighting by Jon Buswell. Opening on 13 February and running to 8 June, this limited season was as elegant and as barbed as the fan itself—an object which becomes both symbol and weapon in Wilde’s satire.

A tale of secrets, scandal, and social survival, this revival added a poignant real-life connection: the mother-daughter duo of Redgrave and Richardson played on-stage opposites in a performance that was layered, lyrical, and laced with Wildean wit.

Title: An Ideal Cast for An Ideal Husband
Tags: oscarwildesurscène classiccomedy martinshaw peterhall westendhistory théâtrelondres londontheatre vintagetheatreposter theatreroyalhaymarket wilderevival anidealhusband britishtheatrelegends affichethéâtrale oscarwilde haymarketstage
Description:
A triumph of wit, style, and theatrical craftsmanship — this vintage poster captures The Peter Hall Company’s acclaimed revival of An Ideal Husband at the Theatre Royal Haymarket. Featuring a stellar cast including Martin Shaw, Kate O’Mara, Simon Ward, Dulcie Gray, and Michael Denison, this was Oscar Wilde played with both polish and bite.

Directed by Peter Hall and designed by Carl Toms, the production had dazzled on both Broadway and the West End, returning triumphantly to Haymarket for a strictly limited season. The ensemble cast turned Wilde’s drawing room into a battlefield of secrets, satire, and scandal—making this a landmark in Wildean revivals.

Still proudly displayed in the Haymarket’s lobby, it’s a testament to a golden era of late-20th-century British theatre, where Wilde’s sharp pen met Hall’s equally sharp direction.

This production was originally presented at the Theatre Royal, Haymarket, London from 17 January 1996 to 20 April 1996. It starred Martin Shaw, Michael Denison and Dulcie Gray who all transferred with the production to the Ethel Barrymore Theatre in New York. Nicky Henson later replaced Martin Shaw in the New York production.

Title: Haymarket History – Alan Bates in The Master Builder
Tags: classicdrama posterarttheatre alanbates peterhall dramahistory londontheatrehistory haymarketstage themasterbuilder stagelegends henrikibsen victoriahamilton théâtrelondres ibsenrevival gemmajones westendtheatre theatreroyalhaymarket vintagetheatreposter
Description: A theatrical titan captured in full storm: this vintage poster for The Master Builder by Henrik Ibsen showcases Alan Bates in a powerful 12-week run at Theatre Royal Haymarket, directed by Peter Hall and presented by The Peter Hall Company. With Gemma Jones, Victoria Hamilton, and a cast of stage veterans, this 1990s production brought Ibsen’s drama of ambition, ego, and reckoning back to the London stage with formidable force. Displayed within the Haymarket itself, the poster is a reminder of the theatre’s deep connection to great drama, great actors, and great ambition — all etched in stormy blue and theatrical gravitas. Master Builder at Theatre Royal Haymarket Alan Bates starred in a production of "The Master Builder" by Henrik Ibsen at the Theatre Royal, Haymarket. This production opened on October 13, 1995, and ran until January 6, 1996. Key Details about the Production Cast: Halvard Solness: Alan Bates Hilde Wangel: Victoria Hamilton Aline Solness: Gemma Jones Dr. Herdal: John Normington Director: The play was directed by Peter Hall, who is known for his nuanced approach to classic texts. Reception: The performance was noted for its intense portrayals, particularly Bates's interpretation of Solness, where he conveyed vulnerability and complexity through a unique vocal style. Critics commented on the contrast between the play's subtleties and the director's approach, which sometimes leaned towards the overtly dramatic. About the Theatre Theatre Royal Haymarket is one of London’s oldest theatres, with a history dating back to 1720. It has a rich legacy of presenting significant works, making it an important venue in the West End.

Description:
Taken on the way to an evening stroll through Chinatown & Leicester Square, a chance to do some "street photography", trying out the Viltrox 35mm f1.7 for the first time.
Trafalgar Square is a public square in the City of Westminster in Central London, established in the early 19th century around the area known as Charing Cross. Its name commemorates the Battle of Trafalgar, the British naval victory in the Napoleonic Wars over France and Spain that took place on 21 October 1805 off the coast of Cape Trafalgar, southwest Spain, although it was not named as such until 1835.

Title: Piccadilly People
Description:
This was one I submitted as an entry in the Scott Kelby Worldwide Photo Walk 2025. There was a grand total of four people on our little jaunt in central London and, as far as I am aware, none of the other three bothered to submit any pics at all. 

Photographing well-known landmarks isn’t easy. Especially when you’ can only spend five minutes there. In that short time you have to try to find something new to “say”, line it up, guage exposure and shoot. So getting anything worthwhile is purely a matter of luck.

Overall, I wasn’t totally happy with any of the pics I submitted, including this one, but what the heck, on an event like this participating and having fun is the name of the game.

Description:
BEAUTY < IMMORTALITY

FRANK PICK 1878 - 1941

BEAUTY < IMMORTALITY
UTILITY < PERFECTION
GOODNESS < RIGHTEOUSNESS
TRUTH < WISDOM


Memorial Concept and Design by Ben Langlands & Nikki Bell, 2016.

As the visionary managing director of London Underground in the 1920s and vice chairman of the newly created London Transport from 1933-40, FRANK PICK was instrumental in establishing the most progressive and modern public transport system in the world.

Frank Pick planned the expansion of the bus and Underground network and commissioned the leading architects, artists and designers of the time to shape every aspect of the passenger experience. "London Transport", he said, "is or will be a work of art". From the classic station designs by Charles Holden, to the range of memorable travel posters, from the adoption of the famous Roundel, one of London's most recognisable symbols, to the introduction of Harry Beck's iconic map, and the commissioning of Edward 

Johnston's type-face still in use today, everything was considered by Pick.
Frank Pick's own words above, taken by Langlands & Bell from his lecture notes in the Transport for London archive, demonstrate his conviction that good design contributes decisively to the quality of city life.

Title: First Bus London Limited Optare Metrodecker BEV
Description:
First shall be no more….the sad story of Optare’s struggles in the double-decker market. 

Some of the first BEV double-deckers for London were these buses, such as OME46029 (YJ21EXM). This bus was new to Tower Transit before the formation of the joint venture London Transit operation with RATP Dev but it’s now with First. 

The Metrodecker had a tortured history. It wasn’t by any means the first double-decker built by Optare. Technically the first I suppose were some Leyland Olympians built to Charles H Roe’s design which Leyland allowed them to build whilst Optare got on its feet in 1985. Optare of course was formed out of the ashes of the former Roe business which British Leyland closed as it grappled with losses. These buses went to both West Yorkshire PTE and South Yorkshire PTE. 

Optare went down a different path from other UK builders, pushing one-stop shopping. Rather than going to a bus manufacturer and ordering a chassis and then sourcing a bodybuilder, with Optare you dealt with them all the way. To do this it offered specific buses on a specific chassis. So for example you got the Citypacer sitting on Volkswagen LT55 van chassis, the Delta sitting on a DAF Bus chassis and so on. It did run the risk of alienating customers who wanted a specific chassis it didn’t offer but it appealed to many operators who appreciated the stylish buses and dealing with one company for any issues.

After the small batch of Olympians, Optare didn’t build double-deckers for several years. To be fair this was post deregulation and few fleets were buying them. However with the market picking up towards the end of the 80s, Optate couldn’t ignore this market. 

The demise of MCW provided an opportunity. Optare snapped up the rights to build the Metrobus. However before it built any, it and chassis partner DAF Bus thoroughly went through the Metrobus design, addressing any flaws and improving it. It then relaunched the bus as the Optare Spectra. It sat on a DAF Bus chassis - the DB250 - but the rear owed a lot to the Metrobus. Optare designed a very stylish body for it which featured a front from its Vectra midi-bus and no lower-deck rear window. This was a fad of the time as it was felt the increased rear end would allow more space for advertising. It was a very stylish bus though and it forced other bodybuilders to up their game. 

Sadly the Spectra wasn’t as successful as you may think it was, given how it was positively received at the time. Only 52 were built in its original form. It was badly impacted by the collapse of United Bus, of with Optare and DAF Bus was part. Although Optare was able to extricate itself from the collapse, confidence was dented and orders dried up. 

However the low-floor revolution was coming and double-deckers needed to be part of it. Optare and DAF Bus - the latter now being part of VDL Bus - adapted its DB250 chassis into the DB250LF with a lower floor. Optare amended the body and this became the first low-floor double decker. The low-floor version was more successful with over 200 built for various operators, including some for export to Turkey. It remained in production until 2006 but with DAF and Optare being separated now, DAF Bus could and indeed did make the chassis available to other manufacturers. 

With the ending of Spectra production, it appeared Optare had again left the double-decker market. But in a strange twist of fate, Optare branded double deckers began appearing again. Kind of. 

Optare had been bought and then sold by North American Bus Industries (NABI) which despite the name implying it was across the pond was actually no more American than a plate of goulash. It was a Hungarian company that had designs on being a global player but when that didn’t work out, it sold Optare to a management buyout. 

Enter stage left Jamesstan Investments a part of the Darwen Group. That company owned East Lancashire Coachbuilders and in 2008 it purchased Optare. Then in a sort of reverse takeover it decided to trade as Optare going forward and binned the East Lancs name. It meant buses built at the former East Lancs factory in Blackburn began to have the Optare name added. 

East Lancs offered a variety of different double-decker bodies. It had the Olympus body available on Volvo and Alexander Dennis chassis plus also available on Scania and VDL Bus too. It also still offered the Omni-dekka which was an earlier design on Scania chassis kept in production due to operator pressure. It also offered the Visionaire, which was a purpose built open-top double decker, mainly but not exclusively on Volvo chassis. Tri-axle versions of the Olympus and Visionaire were also available. 

Some rationalisation took place. A facility at Rotherham closed and the former East Lancs Esteem single-decker design ceased in 2009. The double-deckers hung on to 2011 but the plan was that they should have been replaced by a new design.

This was the Rapta. It was to be an integral design and featured a Mercedes-Benz engine and had a look not too dissimilar to an Enviro 400MMC to my eyes. But the vehicle shown featured blacked-out windows as apparently it wasn’t quite finished in time. Also at the same show was a facelifted Optare Solo which featured a similar design look and was called the ‘Solo+’. However such was the negative feedback to the new look Solo that it seems to have doomed the Rapta at the same time. So again Optare exited the double-decker market. 

Again in 2013, Optare announced it would be returning to building double-deckers. The new double-decker, called the Metrodecker, shared bits of the newly launched MetroCity single-decker with a similar design features of this bus. It hoped to deliver the first buses in 2014 with the aim of having 100 in service by the end of that year. 

However the bus had the gestation of a pregnant elephant. It took until 2018 for the first orders to be announced and that was four for Reading Buses for Greenline work. However these were cancelled due ro the COVID pandemic. The buses were eventually built and they joined three demonstrators as the only diesel Metrodeckers built. 

Nothing daunted, Optare announced that a Battery Electric version would be built in 2015. A hybrid version - presumably for London - was also planned but never built. A Hydrogen powered version also was planned but never entered service. 

The electric version was more successful, but not.by much, with three London operators placed orders with Metroline taking 31, Go-Ahead London taking 17 and Tower Transit taking 37 over two batches. Metroline’s came in 2019, Tower Transit’s came in 2020 and Go-Ahead’s in 2021. First York also took 21 for Park and Ride services in York. Delivery of these buses for First York and Tower Transit was painfully slow, due to industrial action by the staff, which in a pandemic takes some doing. A demonstration electric ⚡️ bus was also built, which also ran for a bit in Glasgow during the COP26 summit plus Xplore Dundee too. But it seemed to spend a lot of time at First York covering issues with their buses. 

Two thermal issues with the buses in London saw Optare pull the buses out of service for around a month. This was eventually traced to an issue with the coolant which hadn’t been insulated properly, which probably didn’t inspire confidence in the design. 

No repeat orders were placed. Whilst this was going on it was decided that any electric buses wouldn’t be called Optare moving forward. The company had been bought again, this time by Ashok-Leyland, an Indian company which had been British Leyland’s Indianan Importer. That company had decided to trade as Switch Mobility moving forward but no more Metrodeckers have been built. Optare would remain for the diesel buses but electric ones would be branded as Switch. But it was not to be.,With Ashkok-Leyland now announcing the closure of its British operations, it seems that the Metrodecker will be the last ‘new’ double-decker launched by what was Optare. Which is quite sad.,

Title: comyn ching 04
Tags: london terryfarrell postmodern coventgarden sevendials 19811991 chingcourt comynching
Description:
Terry Farrell died a couple of days ago, so I looked in at the Comyn Ching block in Covent Garden while shopping nearby. The block consists of restored 18th century buildings, with new corner pavilions by Farrell in a postmodern style. Farrell also designed new doorcases and landscaping in the courtyard.

Note the use of red accents, including the windows above the courtyard entrance on the left.

Title: comyn ching 01
Tags: london terryfarrell postmodern coventgarden sevendials 19811991 chingcourt comynching
Description:
Terry Farrell died a couple of days ago, so I looked in at the Comyn Ching block in Covent Garden while shopping nearby. The block consists of restored 18th century buildings, with new corner pavilions by Farrell in a postmodern style. Farrell also designed new doorcases and landscaping in the courtyard.

The Seven Dials sundial column was reinstated in 1989 after a two-century absence. The corner building on the left is part of the Comyn Ching block by Farrell. I'm not sure if he also did the one on the right.

Title: comyn ching 03
Tags: london terryfarrell postmodern coventgarden sevendials 19811991 chingcourt comynching
Description:
Terry Farrell died a couple of days ago, so I looked in at the Comyn Ching block in Covent Garden while shopping nearby. The block consists of restored 18th century buildings, with new corner pavilions by Farrell in a postmodern style. Farrell also designed new doorcases and landscaping in the courtyard.

Unfortunately this corner had its full-height triangular bay window replaced by flat windows and balconies in 2016 - thus losing the sharpness of the point. As at Landmark House, postmodern details are vulnerable to the big-windows fashion. I don't see why the balconies couldn't have been pointed to retain the idea.

The building on the right is a 1950s response to the same context.

Title: Queen Elizabeth I and William Sidney (courtier) - Tudor Gallery at National Portrait Gallery
Tags: cityofwestminster london greaterlondon england unitedkingdom greatbritain nationalportraitgallery npg charingcrossrd stmartinsplace trafalgarsquare gradeilistedbuilding gradeilisted ewanchristian jkcolling portlandstoneslateleadandglazedroofs freelycomposeditalianrenaissancedesign stmartinslane portrait tudorgallery queenelizabethi williamsidneycourtier
Description: A visit to the National Portrait Gallery. Was a bag search. Have a feeling I've been once before, but this was the first time I got some photos of the portraits inside the gallery. Once there, we got the lift to the top floor, and proceeded to down to each floor looking at the portraits. The National Portrait Gallery (NPG) is an art gallery in London that houses a collection of portraits of historically important and famous British people. When it opened in 1856, it was arguably the first national public gallery in the world that was dedicated to portraits. The gallery moved in 1896 to its current site at St Martin's Place, off Trafalgar Square, and adjoining the National Gallery. The National Portrait Gallery also has regional outposts at Beningbrough Hall in Yorkshire and Montacute House in Somerset. It is unconnected to the Scottish National Portrait Gallery in Edinburgh, with which its remit overlaps. The gallery is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department for Culture, Media and Sport. Grade I listed building National Portrait Gallery Tudor Gallery Queen Elizabeth I and William Sidney (courtier)

The National Portrait Gallery (NPG) is an art gallery in London that houses a collection of portraits of historically important and famous British people. When it opened in 1856, it was arguably the first national public gallery in the world that was dedicated to portraits. The gallery moved in 1896 to its current site at St Martin's Place, off Trafalgar Square, and adjoining the National Gallery. The National Portrait Gallery also has regional outposts at Beningbrough Hall in Yorkshire and Montacute House in Somerset. It is unconnected to the Scottish National Portrait Gallery in Edinburgh, with which its remit overlaps. The gallery is a non-departmental public body sponsored by the Department for Culture, Media and Sport.
